---
title: "`escalc` cheatsheet"
subtitle: "so many estimators, so little time"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
format: 
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: right
    toc-depth: 6
    toc-float: true
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: "**CONTENTS**"
    output-file: "escalc_cheatsheet.html"
    # embed-resources: true
    # code-fold: true
    code-link: true
    code-tools: true
    # number-sections: false
    # bibliography: ./bib/ref.bib
    fontsize: "10"
    mainfont: "Helvetica"
    monofont: "Fira Code"
    # fontcolor: ""
    page-layout: article
    code-overflow: wrap
    df_print: paged
    theme: cosmo
    code-line-numbers: false
    grid:
      margin-width: 250px
crossref:
  fig-title: Figure     # (default is "Figure")
  tbl-title: Table     # (default is "Table")
  title-delim: "—"     # (default is ":")
  fig-prefix: Fig.     # (default is "Figure")
  tbl-prefix: Table.   # (default is "Table")
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  tidy: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false
#| out-width: "70%"

rm(list = ls())
library("metaDigitise")
library("ggplot2")
library("gt")
library("lubridate")
library("data.table")
library("ggpattern")
library("patchwork")
library("metafor")
library("dplyr")
library("broom")
library("broom.mixed")
library("simplermarkdown")
library("crayon")

# Source our custom functions:
source("scripts/functions/eff_size.R")
source("scripts/functions/convert_effect_sizes.R")

```

# SMD and lnRoM options

*I wrote this when i thought these alternative formulations were comparable with SMD. Now that I know they are not, I'm not sure about having this here...Might move to appendix*

The measurement options in `escalc` are "SMCC", "SMCR", and "SMCRP". Two of these have a heteroscedastic version ("SMCRH", "SMCRPH"). Given the diversity of formulations of these effect sizes, we've organized the options available in `escalc` into a table. We discuss these options in further depth below.

So should use a paired formulation that doesn't affect point estimate, just variance. The one we have in eff_size is that and you presumably could use it with BACI...

| class     | `escalc` | Heteroscedasticity | Paired designs | Details |
|----------|-----------|----------|----------|-----------------------------------|
| SMD       | "SMD"     | No      | No | The default and most commonly used estimator, formulated as "Hedges' g" (REF). |
|  | "SMDH" | Yes | No | A formulation of Hedges' g that can account for heteroscedasticity between treatment groups. |
|  | "SMCC" | No | Yes | Paired group formulation that uses the standard deviation of group 1 and 2. |
|  | "SMCR" | No | Yes | Paired group formulation that uses standard deviation of group 1 only. |
|  | "SMCRH" | Yes | Yes | Same as above but for heteroscedastic data. |
|  | "SMCRP" | No | Yes | An alternative formulation that uses the standard deviations of both group 1 and 2. |
| lnRoM | "ROM" | No | No | The default log-transformed ratio of means. |
|  | "ROMC" | No | Yes | Paired formula for lnRoM. |

Note that "Zmu" is only available in our own function `eff_this` (originally published in Nakagawa et al. XXXX) and supplied here. To select a paired formula, set paired_design = TRUE and be sure to provide `r` and `n` as an input.

**Look into this:** "See also Morris and DeShon (2002) for a thorough discussion of the difference between the "SMCC" and "SMCR" change score measures"

**Not sure when you should use SMCC versus SMCRP**
**I don't think any of them are compatible with SMD unpaired...Gahhh**
